children.title: Using 'isin' Function in Pandas for Filtering
children.summary: The 'isin' function in Pandas checks if elements are present in a specified list, returning a boolean array of True/False values. This can be applied to a DataFrame column, such as 'Name', to filter data based on a list of names (e.g., Bella, Alex, Nargis, Lisa). The output allows for context-dependent extraction using square bracket notation, simplifying the process of filtering data. Additionally, there are various string functions in Pandas that can be utilized for processing string values in DataFrame columns.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the first function that I'm going to show you is dot is in. So dot is in the, as you can see from the name, and pandas is really readable, and the API is really kind of... Velh, chosen those function names really Velh, it basically says that it's going to check whether something is in something else, right? So how can I make use of this? The way that I can make use of this is that I can say that I'm going to put all of those names that I wanted, remember Bella, Alex, Nargis and Lisa, into a list. And then what I can do is I can go ahead and say that on the baby names. Name column on that series object Check whether those elements are in The name's the list that I have. So very simple, the output is going to be, again, the inner side is going to basically check whether it is in or not, true false, true false, true false, and then I can pass it to the context dependent extraction. This is square bracket operator and then it will do the actual task of filtering for us. So that's also another easier, higher level way of checking condition or creating more complex condition. There are also a lot of string functions. So I'm going to just show you, I think a couple of string functions today, because a lot of times you have string values in the columns and you want to process them some way. Video: 1: -

children.title: String Functions and DataFrame Modifications in Pandas
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of string functions in the Pandas library, specifically the 'startswith' function, which checks if strings in a series begin with a specified character. The output is a Boolean series that can be used for indexing to filter names starting with a particular letter. Additionally, it mentions that while filtering is important, there are also various methods available in Pandas for modifying DataFrames, including functions like .isin and .groupby, which will be covered in future lectures.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But we have a whole lecture on how to process the strings, which I think is going to be lecture. 6. If I'm remembering it correctly. But here, a third of the starts with, it basically says that I'm getting... I'm telling you that this column is of type of string, dot the stair is saying that now I want to invoke a string function, because you think that this is an object, but I'm telling you that this is a string, and then in the stair kind of library, or the stair library, but there is a function that is called starts with. And then it starts with it's going to check whether the string, each one of those strings and that series is start with n or not. And the output here, as you can imagine, the inside is going to be a Boolean series, which then I can index and get the full list of all of the names that is start with n. So there is a whole set of functions that are available in this third library that we can use and we're going to go over them later. So you might be asking that you are talking about selection, you're talking about filtering and whatnot, you did not tell us how we can modify it. So if someone hands us a data, we're just basically limited to just filtering it and just taking a look at the filtered version. You didn't talk about how we can modify it. And in fact, there are different easy ways to modify a data frame. Video: 1: v

children.title: DataFrame Column Operations in Pandas
children.summary: Adding a column to a DataFrame in Pandas involves specifying a key (column name) and an array of values that matches the DataFrame's length, similar to a dictionary. Existing columns can be used to derive new values, such as calculating name lengths. Modifying a column is done by referencing it with square brackets and assigning a new Series or array, which can overwrite existing content. The .rename() method allows renaming columns using a dictionary that maps old names to new ones. To drop rows or columns, the .drop() method is used, with the axis specified for columns.

children.title: Adding a Column to a DataFrame in Pandas
children.summary: Adding a column in a DataFrame is similar to adding a key-value pair in a dictionary. To create a new column, specify the key (column name) and provide an array of values that matches the length of the DataFrame. The values can be derived from existing columns, such as calculating the length of names in a 'name' column to create a 'baby_name_lengths' series. This series can then be appended to the DataFrame as a new column, which will automatically appear on the right side of the existing columns. The order of columns can be adjusted, but typically they are referenced by labels.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We can easily add a column. So adding a column is as easy as adding a key value pair in a dictionary. So how did you add a key value pair in a dictionary? You would just automatically say that I have this key and then provide the value. So it works exactly the same way here. We would say that I am creating this new column. Just provide the key, provide the name. And I'm saying that the values that you're going to store in this column is going to be coming from this series object. So you have to provide kind of an array type value of the same length as the length of the initial data frame and then it will automatically append that to the entire data frame that you have. But where did that baby name underscore? Langs came from. This is what I calculated at the top here. And the way that I calculated is basically that I got the name column. I said that this name column is a string and get me the Langs of each individual one of them. So this function is going to go through the name column, get calculated lengths of each one of them. It creates a series object. It stores the series object into this baby name, underscore, Lenses. And what I just need to do is to make sure that there is the correct reference added to the data frame. I'm just directly saying that create a new key, and this is its value, and it will automatically be added as an additional column to the baby names, data frame, and you can see that it's added to the right-hand side. It's always by default is going to depend from the end. so that it knows how many columns you add that if you don't provide and then it basically goes from there and you can also There are different ways that you can shuffle columns if you want to but there's no point because you're always referring to them most of the time with labels and that's all that matters So, you add a column, so that's good. leaf_chunk.audio: We can easily add a column. So adding a column is as easy as adding a key value pair in a dictionary. So how did you add a key value pair in a dictionary? You would just automatically say that I have this key and then provide the value. So it works exactly the same way here. We would say that I am creating this new column. Just provide the key, provide the name. And I'm saying that the values that you're going to store in this column is going to be coming from this series object. So you have to provide kind of an array type value of the same length as the length of the initial data frame and then it will automatically append that to the entire data frame that you have. But where did that baby name underscore? Langs came from. This is what I calculated at the top here. And the way that I calculated is basically that I got the name column. I said that this name column is a string and get me the Langs of each individual one of them. So this function is going to go through the name column, get calculated lengths of each one of them. It creates a series object. It stores the series object into this baby name, underscore, Lenses. And what I just need to do is to make sure that there is the correct reference added to the data frame. I'm just directly saying that create a new key, and this is its value, and it will automatically be added as an additional column to the baby names, data frame, and you can see that it's added to the right-hand side. It's always by default is going to depend from the end. so that it knows how many columns you add that if you don't provide and then it basically goes from there and you can also There are different ways that you can shuffle columns if you want to but there's no point because you're always referring to them most of the time with labels and that's all that matters So, you add a column, so that's good.

children.title: Modifying DataFrame Columns in Pandas
children.summary: To modify a column in a DataFrame, use the syntax similar to adding a column. Reference the existing column with [ ] and assign it a new Series or array of the appropriate length. If the column does not exist, it will be created; if it does exist, the new values will overwrite the existing content. An example provided involves adjusting calculated name lengths by removing a space from each value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What if you have a data frame, you add it a column or not, but now you want to modify it. You want to say that, oh, I calculated the name lengths, but maybe the name lengths that I calculated has a space in it. So I need to remove one from each one of those values that I had. So in the same way, we can do exactly the same thing. We can say that, okay, now, remember that I had this Name Lenses. Call them. Go ahead and grab the value for it from the value that I already had just reduced it by one. So again, every time that you're referring to a column like this. If it doesn't exist, it will create one. It will assign the value from whatever you're providing in the right hand side. If it does exist, whatever you're providing in the right hand side is going to overwrite its existing content. leaf_chunk.audio: What if you have a data frame, you add it a column or not, but now you want to modify it. You want to say that, oh, I calculated the name lengths, but maybe the name lengths that I calculated has a space in it. So I need to remove one from each one of those values that I had. So in the same way, we can do exactly the same thing. We can say that, okay, now, remember that I had this Name Lenses. Call them. Go ahead and grab the value for it from the value that I already had just reduced it by one. So again, every time that you're referring to a column like this. If it doesn't exist, it will create one. It will assign the value from whatever you're providing in the right hand side. If it does exist, whatever you're providing in the right hand side is going to overwrite its existing content.

children.title: Modifying Columns in Pandas DataFrames
children.summary: Modifying a column in a Pandas DataFrame is similar to adding a new column. To modify an existing column, use square brackets to reference the column and assign it a new Series or array of the appropriate length. For example, to decrease the values in the 'name_lengths' column by one, the syntax would be: babynames['name_lengths'] = babynames['name_lengths'] - 1.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right? So that's exactly how dictionaries work. And this is exactly how it works in pandas. So here all of the name blanks is reduced by one and you can verify because mere is for characters and now it says trees so we correctly modified it. What if I give you a data frame or you create a name for a data frame or a column and you're not happy with the name that we gave to that column. Maybe the label is too long. You don't want to keep typing name, underscore, and length. Video: 1: v

children.title: Using .rename() Method in Pandas
children.summary: The .rename() method in Pandas is used to rename columns in a DataFrame. It requires a dictionary that maps old column names to the new names. For example, if a column is currently named 'name_lengths', it can be renamed to 'Length' by specifying this mapping in the dictionary.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And you want to rename it to something else. You can use dot rename and you can provide a dictionary that dictionary is structured as all of those previous names or old names that you had and then the corresponding current name that you want to assign it to. So for example here I would say that okay I have baby names, I want to rename it and then and what I'm renaming is that I'm renaming columns. And then the previous value that I had for one column was named underscore Lenses and the new value that I want to sign or the new label is going to be Lenses. children.title: Dropping Rows and Columns in Pandas
children.summary: To drop rows or columns in a Pandas DataFrame, use the .drop() method. By default, .drop() removes rows, so specify the axis for columns. For example, to drop a column, indicate the column axis; otherwise, it will attempt to drop a row and may result in a key error. You can drop multiple rows or columns simultaneously by providing their labels.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: or likes. And you can rename multiple columns at the same time. So you just add another element to this dictionary, and then you should be able to rename multiple columns all at once. In this case, the output is just going to be that now I have length column instead of... Name underscored lenses. Okay, what if I want to drop a row or I want to drop a colon? By default, pandas things that you want to drop a row. So you call .drop. It will go ahead and drop a row for you. And you will specify which row you want to drop. If you want to drop a column, you should specifically say that the axis that I'm dropping is the column axis. And then this way you can drop a column. So again, you can drop a row, you can drop a column, and this is a way that you can remove content from your data frame. So here I would say .drop. And I'm saying that the label that you're dropping is lengths. Remember that this was a column. But if I don't say that this is from the columns axis, it will try to remove it using a label from a row. You can find it, it will give you a key error. So that's why you have to make sure that you tell it that this is a column that then dropping, because the default goes by rows. If you want to drop a row, you pass a label for the row, and then it will be able to drop a row, or you can also drop multiple rows at the same time. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

children.title: Using drop() Method in Pandas for DataFrames
children.summary: The segment explains how to use the drop() method in Pandas to remove columns or rows from a DataFrame. It highlights that by default, drop() removes rows, and to drop a column, the axis parameter must be set to 'columns'. An example is provided where the 'Length' column is dropped from the 'babynames' DataFrame. Additionally, it emphasizes that modifications create a copy of the DataFrame unless explicitly reassigned, and using the 'inplace' parameter allows changes to be applied directly to the original DataFrame.

leaf_chunk.text:

This is because every time that I modify or drop the stuff is going to be saved into a copy. It's not going to overwrite my original data frame. So I had to specifically say that overwrite my baby names with this new version that has that column drop and then it will do that for me. device will create a copy and what you're seeing is that if you say dot drop and then you print or plot whatever the content is, you will still see it existing there. So either you re-assigned directly in the same way that we did before or you could say in place. be called to true. If you say in place, if all to true, that means that like do it in place. children.title: Utility Functions in Pandas for Data Extraction
children.summary: The segment discusses utility functions in pandas for data extraction, specifically filtering data using Boolean expressions to find entries where the name is 'Yash'. It explains how to use indexing to count these entries, highlighting the importance of double square brackets for column selection and creating a series object with the counts. Additionally, it covers the flexibility of indexing methods such as .loc and .iloc.

children.title: Utility Functions in Pandas for Data Extraction
children.summary: The segment discusses utility functions in pandas for data extraction, focusing on filtering data with Boolean expressions to extract entries where the name is 'Yash'. It explains indexing to count these entries, emphasizing the use of double square brackets for column selection and creating a series object with the counts. Additionally, it covers the flexibility of indexing methods such as .loc and .iloc.

children.title: Utility Functions in Pandas for Data Extraction
children.summary: The segment discusses various utility functions available in pandas, particularly focusing on data extraction techniques. It demonstrates how to filter data using Boolean expressions, specifically extracting entries where the name is 'Yash'. The explanation includes indexing to obtain a count of these entries, highlighting the use of double square brackets for selecting columns and the creation of a series object with the counts. The segment also touches on the flexibility of indexing with methods like .loc and .iloc.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: 3 Okay, next piece, some utility functions, as if I haven't covered enough of a different functions. Now I'm going to talk about what are the different utility functions that are available in pandas. All the fun things. NumPy, everything NumPy is available to you. So say for example, here I would go ahead and just look at all of the babies with name Yash. How did I do that? Here I am doing the context. I'm doing the Boolean expression here that the name is equal to Yash, so it gives me all of those Yash babies, right? And it's a data frame with all of those. And I'm only interested in the count of them. So that's why you see that I concadred this additional selection that if you think about this as a data frame on top of that now, I'm also indexing and getting only one column. So this is context dependent extraction and extracting only a single value which was extracting a column. This is the first time that you're seeing two escrow brackets next to each other. This is not like a 2D array thing that you'd seen in different places or like in Java or things like that. But this is like that. I created a data frame. Now I have a 9, keep indexing into it, indexing into it. So you can keep adding those escrow brackets next to each other or like dot lock and dot i lock back to back. children.title: NumPy and Pandas Basics: Size and Shape
children.summary: The segment discusses the functionalities of NumPy and Pandas, particularly in calculating mean and maximum values of data series. It explains the use of the 'size' and 'shape' attributes of a DataFrame to determine the number of rows and columns, with .shape providing dimensions as (number of rows, number of columns) and .size indicating the total number of entries. An example using the 'babynames' DataFrame shows it has 407428 rows and 5 columns. Additionally, the .describe() function is introduced, which offers summary statistics for numerical columns, including count, average, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum values, aiding in identifying missing data.

children.title: NumPy and Pandas Basics: Size and Shape
children.summary: The segment covers the functionality of NumPy and Pandas, focusing on calculating mean and maximum values of data series. It explains how to use the 'size' and 'shape' attributes of a DataFrame to determine the number of rows and columns, aiding in understanding the dimensions of the DataFrame. Specifically, .shape returns dimensions as (number of rows, number of columns), while .size gives the total number of entries. An example with the 'babynames' DataFrame illustrates it has 407428 rows and 5 columns. Additionally, the .describe() function provides summary statistics for numerical columns, including count, average, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum values, which helps identify missing data.

children.title: NumPy and Pandas Basics: Size and Shape
children.summary: The segment discusses the functionality of NumPy and Pandas, particularly focusing on operations like calculating the mean and maximum values of data series. It explains how to use the 'size' and 'shape' attributes of a DataFrame to determine the number of rows and columns, which helps in understanding the dimensions and capacity of the data frame.

I gave you kind of a blurb of unique. I gave you the blurb of, I think, shape once. But let's take a look at them quickly. If I have a data frame when I say dot size, that dot shape, it will give you the number of rows. and it will give you the number of columns. Right? So that's easy for you to figure out how many rows do you have if you want to provide this slice and like get this slice and making sure that you're within the range. So that's an easy way to get the dimensions or have a sense of the dimensions or the size of the data. :

T

ab
In addition to its rich syntax for indexing and support for other libraries (NumPy, native Python
1035326
functions), pandas provides an enormous number of useful utility functions. Today, we'll
discuss just a few:
size/shape
describe
sample
value_counts
unique
sort_values
The pandas library is rich in utility functions (we could spend the entire summer talking about
them)! We encourage you to explore as you complete your assignments by Googling and
reading documentation, just as data scientists do.

I gave you kind of a blurb of unique. I gave you the blurb of, I think, shape once. But let's take a look at them quickly. If I have a data frame when I say dot size, that dot shape, it will give you the number of rows. and it will give you the number of columns. Right? So that's easy for you to figure out how many rows do you have if you want to provide this slice and like get this slice and making sure that you're within the range. So that's an easy way to get the dimensions or have a sense of the dimensions or the size of the data. If you're interested in just knowing how many entries could possibly be in your data frame, you could just call that size. That size is technically the capacity of the data frame.

children.title: Using .shape and .size in Pandas
children.summary: .shape returns the dimensions of a DataFrame or Series as (number of rows, number of columns), while .size gives the total number of entries (rows times columns). The example with the 'babynames' DataFrame shows it has 407428 rows and 5 columns. The .describe() function provides summary statistics for numerical columns, including count, average, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum values, helping to identify any missing data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's number of rows times number of columns. And if you don't trust me, if you multiply 5 by that 400,000 thing, this is the number that you'll get. So it just doesn't care about the values that the restore just is a capacity thing. Describe is going to give you when I call this scribe on a data frame. It's going to only look at the numerical columns. So here the numerical columns that I have is year and account. Right? And it will give me some summary statistic about those columns. So it will give me okay. What's the count? How many actual values do I have? do I have because you might have missing values. It will give me like average standard deviation minimum maximum. children.title: Using describe() and .sample() in Pandas; Sorting Values
children.summary: The describe() function in Pandas provides summary statistics for DataFrames or Series, including count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and quartiles, aiding in visualizing distributions and understanding dataset characteristics, exemplified with the 'babynames' dataset. The .sample() method allows for random sampling from a DataFrame, with options for sampling with or without replacement, specifying sample size, and applying conditions for filtering. Additionally, sorting values in Pandas is addressed, emphasizing the .sort_values() method for Series and DataFrames, where strings are sorted alphabetically by default but can be sorted in descending order. The .unique() method retrieves unique values, and for DataFrames, sorting can be performed by one or multiple columns, illustrated by sorting by the 'count' column. Custom sorting is also covered, including creating a temporary 'name_lengths' column for sorting by name length and using key arguments with lambda functions for custom criteria.

children.title: Using describe() and .sample() in Pandas
children.summary: The describe() function in Pandas provides summary statistics for DataFrames or Series, including count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and quartiles, which helps visualize distributions and understand dataset characteristics, as seen with the 'babynames' dataset. The .sample() method allows for random sampling of data from a DataFrame, with default behavior being without replacement, but can be set to sample with replacement by adjusting the 'replace' parameter. It enables specifying the number of samples and applying further indexing and filtering based on conditions, such as sampling entries from a specific year.

children.title: Using describe() for DataFrame Summary Statistics
children.summary: The describe() function provides summary statistics for a DataFrame or Series, including count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and quartiles. In the context of the 'babynames' dataset, it reveals insights such as the maximum year of data (2022), indicating that any births after this year are not included. The function helps visualize distributions and understand the dataset's characteristics, which will be further explored in upcoming lectures.

children.title: Using describe() in Pandas for Data Analysis
children.summary: The dot describe() method in Pandas provides summary statistics for a DataFrame or Series, including the count of elements, number of unique values, and the most frequent entries. For example, when applied to a Series object representing baby names, it reveals that female names are more frequent, with specific counts. The method outputs various statistics such as mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum, and quartiles, aiding in understanding the distribution of data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So these are all the sanity checkers stuff that we want to do before getting into feature in generating and modeling and whatnot. If I call dot describe on a series object, so here I'm getting the series object only one column and just calling the dot describe in this case, but I have is Something is slightly different, but it just describes something and that's how pandas is implemented So what you're seeing here What you are seeing? Okay, this is a work. time maybe. It tells me how many elements I have. It still gives me count. It tells me how many unique elements I got there. And then it will tell me what is the top frequency of those unique things that I have. We know that it's F and M. And it tells me that F or female babies are more frequent in the data frame. And then it will tell me that the frequency of female babies is this much. So that's how it's implemented. We can also sample from a data frame. Video: 1: V

children.title: Sampling Data Frames with .sample Method
children.summary: The .sample method is used to sample data from a data frame, with the default behavior being sampling without replacement, meaning the same entry can be selected multiple times. To sample with replacement, the 'replace' parameter can be set to true. The method allows for specifying the number of samples, such as .sample(5) to retrieve five entries. After sampling, further indexing can be applied to select specific rows and columns, such as starting from a certain column index. Additionally, filtering can be performed based on conditions, such as sampling only entries from the year 2000, emphasizing the importance of understanding the order of operations when applying filters and sampling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So there is a whole set of lectures that we're going to talk about sampling a data frame and just looking at the distribution and whatnot. So in order to sample data from a data frame, we can use .sample. So very creative. Without sample it always assumes that sampling is without replacement. So there is a chance that you sample multiple times back to back and then you will be able to sample the same thing or you sample five entries and then you get one entry twice. So it is with replacement by D for it is without replace sorry it's not a rear on if it's without replacement. That means that you won't get the same int or same same data multiple times. but you can set that replace equal to true so you make it with replacement, which in that case you should be able to extract same entry multiple points. Then you can also apply a lock and I like and all those things on top of that and then get more useful information. So if I call dot sample randomly we'll give me one sample. If I call dot sample 5 and provide an argument here this means that I've won five elements. So sample 5 entries for me. And then on top of that, I'm saying that, okay, now that you gave me this data frame, but five samples, I want to index into it. I want to index into it and then say that I want all the rows, so keep all of those five entries that you gave me, and then give me columns, starting from column at integer position 2 all the way to the end. Which technically means that if this is 0, this is 1, this is 2 all the way to the end means year, name and count are going to be included as you're seeing here. We can go more complex and keep adding stuff on top of each other. I can say that let's first go ahead and filter these babies so that I'm only looking at the interest in the year 2000, right? And then sample from the babies in the year 2000. So a lot of time when we tell you a certain condition, you need to think about what makes sense and what order you should apply stuff, right? leaf_chunk.audio: So there is a whole set of lectures that we're going to talk about sampling a data frame and just looking at the distribution and whatnot. So in order to sample data from a data frame, we can use .sample. So very creative. Without sample it always assumes that sampling is without replacement. So there is a chance that you sample multiple times back to back and then you will be able to sample the same thing or you sample five entries and then you get one entry twice. So it is with replacement by D for it is without replace sorry it's not a rear on if it's without replacement. That means that you won't get the same int or same same data multiple times. but you can set that replace equal to true so you make it with replacement, which in that case you should be able to extract same entry multiple points. Then you can also apply a lock and I like and all those things on top of that and then get more useful information. So if I call dot sample randomly we'll give me one sample. If I call dot sample 5 and provide an argument here this means that I've won five elements. So sample 5 entries for me. And then on top of that, I'm saying that, okay, now that you gave me this data frame, but five samples, I want to index into it. I want to index into it and then say that I want all the rows, so keep all of those five entries that you gave me, and then give me columns, starting from column at integer position 2 all the way to the end. Which technically means that if this is 0, this is 1, this is 2 all the way to the end means year, name and count are going to be included as you're seeing here. We can go more complex and keep adding stuff on top of each other. I can say that let's first go ahead and filter these babies so that I'm only looking at the interest in the year 2000, right? And then sample from the babies in the year 2000. So a lot of time when we tell you a certain condition, you need to think about what makes sense and what order you should apply stuff, right?

children.title: Sampling Rows from DataFrames in Pandas
children.summary: The .sample() method is used to randomly select rows from a DataFrame. By default, it samples without replacement, but setting replace=True allows for sampling with replacement. This method can be combined with other operations, such as iloc, to filter data. An example is provided where the DataFrame 'babynames' is filtered for the year 2000 and then sampled.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that you're sampling from the right distribution. So if you were like doing the filtering afterwards. Then your sampling was not going to be correct. So here in order to make sure that my sampling frame is correct, I'm filtering based on getting the year equal to 2000. Then what I'm doing is that I'm saying that sample for instances would for me and then replacement is allowed in this case. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

children.title: Sampling DataFrames with .sample() Method
children.summary: The .sample() method is used to randomly select rows from a DataFrame, with the default behavior being sampling without replacement. To sample with replacement, the parameter replace=True should be used. This method can be chained with other methods and operators, such as iloc, to further manipulate the data. An example is provided where specific columns, like Year and Count, are selected after sampling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it does it with replacement. After you're done, after you gave me four samples from the year 2000, then I'm doing an additional thing where I'm saying that, Give me all of those rows, but only give me the same columns, the year name and count, and this is how it's going to look like. Okay, so we can keep adding these functions together. I think I've sped it enough. You know what? We do it. Value accounts. So a value account applies to series objects. So when we're calling value accounts on a specific column, the way that it will work is that it will calculate The frequency of each unique occurrences in your in-dad the specific series object. children.title: Using value_counts in Pandas for Unique Names
children.summary: The value_counts method in Pandas is used to calculate the frequency of each unique value in a Series. It counts how many times each name appears, which is distinct from a simple count of occurrences. An example demonstrates that the method returns a Series showing the number of times each name appears, along with an explanation that unique values can also be retrieved without frequency counts using the unique method.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example when I say name.value accounts, it will basically go ahead and calculate or find out all of the unique names that we have. So these are all unique. There is a note that this frequency thing is different than count, right? Every row which had a count is counted as one occurrence. And then we're just counting how many times they appear, which technically here means that I think it means that how many years was it that a baby with this name? BuzzForn. Right? Kind of sort of. because that 223 is a very large number. But anyhow, we can also be seen unique, unique as just that, like if you don't care about the frequencies and you care about the values, you should just call unique and then it will give you an array with all of those unique names. children.title: Sorting Values and Custom Sorting in Pandas
children.summary: The segment covers sorting values in Pandas Series and DataFrames, highlighting the importance of sorting even when data appears sorted. It introduces the .sort_values() method for both Series and DataFrames, explaining that strings are sorted alphabetically by default but can be sorted in descending order. The .unique() method is mentioned for retrieving unique values. For DataFrames, sorting can be done by one or multiple columns, with an example of sorting by the 'count' column to display frequent names. Custom sorting is discussed, including sorting by name length by creating a temporary 'name_lengths' column. The use of key arguments for custom sorting criteria, such as using a lambda function for string lengths, is also explained.

children.title: Sorting Values in Pandas Series and DataFrames
children.summary: The segment discusses how to sort values in Pandas, emphasizing that one should always sort data even if it is claimed to be sorted. The method .sort_values() is introduced, which sorts values in a Series or DataFrame according to the data type. For strings, sorting is done in alphabetical order by default (ascending), but can be changed to descending by setting the parameter ascending to false. The segment also briefly mentions the .unique() method, which returns an array of unique values in a Series.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's what I did when I wanted to make sure that there is a true instance there that the babies our name Joy for Frank and then we moved on. We can actually go ahead and sort values. So remember that I said with the movie earning thing, if you sort them then you can get the median earning. So a lot of time if you want to do something like that. And if they tell you that the data is sorted, you just sorted again, don't trust them. Or if it's not sorted, you still sorted and do your own thing. So, sort value, when it's called on series, it's very straightforward. It will automatically sort all of those values based on the expectation of how sorting works for that data type. So for example, for it, a string sorting works in alphabetical ordering. So all of those letters are going to be ordered alphabetically in alphabetical ordering. So here sort values you see, we're starting from Aidan and then go all the way to the names that are starting with Z. So by default the order is in ascending order, not in the sending order. And if you want the order to be descending you have to set the Set the parameter that you have say ascending false so that it source them descendingly. leaf_chunk.audio: So that's what I did when I wanted to make sure that there is a true instance there that the babies our name Joy for Frank and then we moved on. We can actually go ahead and sort values. So remember that I said with the movie earning thing, if you sort them then you can get the median earning. So a lot of time if you want to do something like that. And if they tell you that the data is sorted, you just sorted again, don't trust them. Or if it's not sorted, you still sorted and do your own thing. So, sort value, when it's called on series, it's very straightforward. It will automatically sort all of those values based on the expectation of how sorting works for that data type. So for example, for it, a string sorting works in alphabetical ordering. So all of those letters are going to be ordered alphabetically in alphabetical ordering. So here sort values you see, we're starting from Aidan and then go all the way to the names that are starting with Z. So by default the order is in ascending order, not in the sending order. And if you want the order to be descending you have to set the Set the parameter that you have say ascending false so that it source them descendingly. If we want to sort a data frame, that's a little bit complicated. Again, here's when the logic is going to be very helpful.

children.title: Sorting DataFrames in Pandas
children.summary: To sort a DataFrame in Pandas, specify the column to sort by using the 'sort_values' method. You can sort by a single column or multiple columns, and can choose to sort in ascending or descending order. For example, sorting by the 'count' column allows you to see the most frequent names at the top. While it's not necessary to memorize all functionalities, it's important to be aware of them for practical use in assignments. Additionally, creativity in data manipulation is encouraged, rather than solely relying on built-in functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If you want to sort a data frame, how would you want to sort it? You just need to stay sort by what? Right? Because you have a bunch of different columns. So that's the only difference. Here you should say, sort by what? And then it will go ahead and sort by that thing that you said. So here I'm saying that, baby names is my data frame. Sort values. Sort by the count column. You can provide two columns. You say, sort by this person. Sort by this other one second. That can be complicated, but there is functionality in pandas. And then here we're saying that the sending files. I don't want it to sending. I want it to be descending so that at the top I see the most frequent kind of years that any name. had appeared which looks like that there was a substantial number of babies named Michael in 1950s and that's the maximum like frequency kind of at least. So you can do all these fancy stuff with the with data frames and all of those functionalities. Again, we don't want you to remember them, but we just want you to have heard about them so that you can keep using them in the lava homework and then you will. You know that they exist. You can go back to the API, find them and then use them. So I want to quickly show you that we can get creative. We do not need to rely on the on pandas to do all the things for us. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: If you want to sort a data frame, how would you want to sort it? You just need to stay sort by what? Right? Because you have a bunch of different columns. So that's the only difference. Here you should say, sort by what? And then it will go ahead and sort by that thing that you said. So here I'm saying that, baby names is my data frame. Sort values. Sort by the count column. You can provide two columns. You say, sort by this person. Sort by this other one second. That can be complicated, but there is functionality in pandas. And then here we're saying that the sending files. I don't want it to sending. I want it to be descending so that at the top I see the most frequent kind of years that any name. had appeared which looks like that there was a substantial number of babies named Michael in 1950s and that's the maximum like frequency kind of at least. So you can do all these fancy stuff with the with data frames and all of those functionalities. Again, we don't want you to remember them, but we just want you to have heard about them so that you can keep using them in the lava homework and then you will. You know that they exist. You can go back to the API, find them and then use them. So I want to quickly show you that we can get creative. We do not need to rely on the on pandas to do all the things for us.

children.title: Custom Sorting by Name Length in DataFrames
children.summary: To perform custom sorts in a DataFrame, one can sort by the length of a name. This involves creating a temporary column to store the length values. By calculating the length of the names in the 'Name' column and assigning these values to a new column named 'name_lengths', the DataFrame can then be sorted based on this new column. This approach allows for flexible sorting techniques.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We can say that, okay, I want to do custom sorts. I don't want to do sorting a sending layer, but same for example, I want to sort by the length of a name. and sort all of those values based on that. So I'm going to just show you, these are like not... things that you haven't seen before, like there's a couple things that you haven't seen before, but these are mostly techniques. So if I want to say that I want to sort my data frame based on the links, but I can do is that go ahead, create a column, name links, and calculate all of those name links values. How do I do that? You had seen it before. I said that go ahead, grab the name column, assume that it's a string, calculate the the length of the story in a series object. Now we run ahead and create it. and then assign those values to that column and then here we're just sorting the data frame by that column that we created. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

children.title: Sorting DataFrames with Key Arguments and Custom Columns
children.summary: The discussion focuses on how to create functionalities for sorting values in a DataFrame using a key argument, which allows for custom sorting criteria. The key argument can utilize a lambda function to determine the order based on string lengths instead of the default alphabetical order. Additionally, a new column can be created to count specific instances (e.g., 'DR' and 'EA') in names, which can then be used to sort the DataFrame based on the frequency of these instances. This approach highlights the flexibility of sorting in data manipulation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Very simple. So this is like for you to think about what functionalities did you know and then how can you create them? And then once you're done with this column you can drop it afterwards. Once you sort of everything you can drop that column and then reassign the values. We can also use a key argument. So here we would say that I want you to sort values and I'm telling you that sort by name. But don't start by name based on the data type of a string that sort's based on alphabetical ordering or the encoding of those strings. But instead I want you to sort it based on this key argument. This key argument says that every time that you saw an instance, the order of this instant is going to be calculated based on that lambda function that we're creating on the life. That Lambda function says that... Once you have a string, once you have a series object, what you're going to do is that assume that it's a string, get the lengths, and that's going to be the priority or order of this string value or that entry. And then it will basically try to sort the entire data frame by the name using the priority that this key argument provided. So think about key as providing the priority that overrides the initial data type priority. And the last fancy way of doing it, which I think is going to have appear in only one of the homeworks, but it will appear somewhere that you don't expect. But it's something that you really need to remember. Is that I can actually go ahead and create a column. So here I'm creating a column, say, DR underscore EA count, because I want to count how many instances of DR and EA exist in each one of those names. And then sort names based on the frequency of the RNA in that, which is kind of crazy, but it's kind of a made up thing to go over this complicated example. So I'm creating this column. I'm saying that the values in this column should be calculated using the name column, initial name, and then what I'll say is that go ahead and use all of those values and apply the function we are underscored EA Count. What is that function? That function basically says that, okay, when you get on a string here, go ahead and count dr, go ahead and count EA, return that value. So what it does, that line of code, is that every instance in each one of the rows, it will go ahead, get that a string, pass it to the function, map that function into that string, Get the corresponding value, store that value into the column that I created. So then I'll have this DREA count that the only thing that I need to do afterwards is just go ahead and say, So, the short values by this, in the sending and sending, and we'll be on our way. leaf_chunk.audio: Very simple. So this is like for you to think about what functionalities did you know and then how can you create them? And then once you're done with this column you can drop it afterwards. Once you sort of everything you can drop that column and then reassign the values. We can also use a key argument. So here we would say that I want you to sort values and I'm telling you that sort by name. But don't start by name based on the data type of a string that sort's based on alphabetical ordering or the encoding of those strings. But instead I want you to sort it based on this key argument. This key argument says that every time that you saw an instance, the order of this instant is going to be calculated based on that lambda function that we're creating on the life. That Lambda function says that... Once you have a string, once you have a series object, what you're going to do is that assume that it's a string, get the lengths, and that's going to be the priority or order of this string value or that entry. And then it will basically try to sort the entire data frame by the name using the priority that this key argument provided. So think about key as providing the priority that overrides the initial data type priority. And the last fancy way of doing it, which I think is going to have appear in only one of the homeworks, but it will appear somewhere that you don't expect. But it's something that you really need to remember. Is that I can actually go ahead and create a column. So here I'm creating a column, say, DR underscore EA count, because I want to count how many instances of DR and EA exist in each one of those names. And then sort names based on the frequency of the RNA in that, which is kind of crazy, but it's kind of a made up thing to go over this complicated example. So I'm creating this column. I'm saying that the values in this column should be calculated using the name column, initial name, and then what I'll say is that go ahead and use all of those values and apply the function we are underscored EA Count. What is that function? That function basically says that, okay, when you get on a string here, go ahead and count dr, go ahead and count EA, return that value. So what it does, that line of code, is that every instance in each one of the rows, it will go ahead, get that a string, pass it to the function, map that function into that string, Get the corresponding value, store that value into the column that I created. So then I'll have this DREA count that the only thing that I need to do afterwards is just go ahead and say, So, the short values by this, in the sending and sending, and we'll be on our way.
